segments.text:
Section 25.15
PEPTIDE BOND FORMATION

Electrophilic Activation of –CO 2 H
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-1
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Formation of acyl chloride with SOCl 2

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-2
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Formation of acyl chloride with SOCl 2 good in theory, but messy

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-3
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Carbodiimides such as dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCCI )

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-4
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Carbodiimides such as dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCCI)
Addition of the acid to DCCI generates a good leaving group, an “ isourea ”
Consider resonance stabilization in the leaving group

Peptide Coupling
The O - acylisourea formed from acid + DCCI reacts with amine in an acyl substitution process to give the amide
The mechanism involves addition followed by elimination (like amination of an acyl chloride)
What is the structure of the byproduct ?

Lengthening the Chain
The dipeptide product is deprotected at nitrogen, exposing a new nucleophile
An N -protected electrophilic amino acid is treated with DCCI, then introduced to the nucleophilic chain
Coupling occurs again!
Rinse and repeat for as many residues as needed
One issue: how to separate byproducts from chain at each step ?

Section 25.16
SOLID-PHASE PEPTIDE SYNTHESIS: THE MERRIFIELD METHOD

Solid-phase Peptide Synthesis
Anchoring the growing polypeptide chain to a solid support or resin enables easy separation of byproducts after each iteration
Since the polypeptide is in the solid phase, this method is called solid-phase peptide synthesis
Vastly expanded the scope and ease of polypeptide synthesis; concept extended to other types of synthesis
Carboxylate of the C -terminal amino acid reacts with the electrophilic resin, establishing the “anchor”

Preparatory Steps
Anchoring to resin
Deprotection of N

Coupling Steps
Peptide coupling
Deprotection of new amine
Repeat these steps to lengthen chain to desired length and sequence .
Isolation of Polypeptide

Section 25.17
SECONDARY STRUCTURES OF PEPTIDES AND PROTEINS

Secondary Structure
Polypeptides exhibit certain long-range conformational patterns; these patterns are called secondary structures
Held in place by hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions between backbone or side-chain groups
The two most important secondary structures are α -helices and β -sheets
In both structures, N–H ––– O=C hydrogen bonding is key

β - Sheets : Planar Structures
Adjacent strands in an anti conformation line up so that C=O groups are across from N–H groups
May be parallel or antiparallel —note positions of the termini

β-Sheets: Planar Structures-1
Adjacent strands in an anti conformation line up so that C=O groups are across from N–H groups
Side chains alternate above and below the plane of the sheet

α -Helices : Tube-like Structures
C=O and N–H groups align “vertically,” resulting in regularly spaced hydrogen bonds
Side chains stick out from the helical core
Most helices are right handed as in this figure ; some are left handed

Overall Protein Structure
Not all amino acid residues are part of α -helices or β -sheets
Large regions of random coils connect these secondary structures
We often see secondary structures arranged in higher-order patterns .

Section 25.18
TERTIARY STRUCTURE OF POLYPEPTIDES AND PROTEINS

Tertiary Structure
Higher-order arrangements of helices and sheets encountered in many proteins are called tertiary structure
A common classification scheme distinguishes globular and fibrous proteins
Where are hydrophobic residues likely to be in globular proteins ?

Tertiary Structure-1
Higher-order arrangements of helices and sheets encountered in many proteins are called tertiary structure
A common classification scheme distinguishes globular and fibrous proteins
Where are hydrophobic residues likely to be in globular proteins ?
The hydrophobic effect causes nonpolar residues to occupy the center of globular proteins.
Polar residues can be found on the periphery .

Origins of Higher-order Structure
Ultimately, the primary sequence of a protein determines its secondary and tertiary structure
Covalent and (mostly) noncovalent interactions between side chains drive the folding of proteins
Predicting the folded state of a protein from its primary sequence remains extremely difficult, but …
We understand the interactions responsible for folding
Interactions in Proteins

Why Understand Folding?
The function of a protein is intimately related to its conformational structure (i.e., how it is folded)
For example, enzymes are proteins that catalyze chemical reactions
Enzymes contain a pocket called the active site where the reaction actually occurs
The shape of the active site and residues within affect stereoselectivity, reaction rate, and product structure

Section 25.19
COENZYMES

Coenzymes: Enzymatic Helpers
The reactivity of enzymatic side chains is limited (more or less) to proton transfers and addition to carbonyls
Coenzymes perform additional chemistry inside active sites
Cofactors are not covalently bound to the enzyme (e.g. NAD + /NADH )
Prosthetic groups are bound to the enzyme (e.g. heme , PLP )
Heme is a critical cofactor in hemoglobin that binds the O 2 we breathe .

Section 26.20
PROTEIN QUATERNARY STRUCTURE: HEMOGLOBIN

Quaternary Structure & Multiple Chains
Some proteins contain multiple polypeptide chains with unique functions
The arrangements of these chains are called the quaternary structure of the protein
For example, hemoglobin includes four chains (two α and two β ) arranged to bind O 2 optimally
Modification of a single residue in patients with sickle-cell anemia leads to critical disruption of quaternary structure

Quaternary Structure & Multiple Chains-1
The X-ray crystal structure of carboxypeptidase A locates this Zn2+ ion in a hydrophobic cavity near the center of the enzyme.
The substrate in the case of carboxypeptidase A is a peptide, especially a peptide with a hydrophobic C-terminal amino acid such as phenylalanine or tyrosine.

Section 25.21
COENZYMES IN REACTIONS OF AMINO ACIDS

Coenzymes in Reactions of Amino Acids
These substances are called prosthetic groups or cofactors , and they can be organic or inorganic. Enzymes that require a prosthetic group for catalytic activity are called coenzymes .
The protein portion of the coenzyme is called the apoenzyme , and the combination of the protein and cofactor is called the holoenzyme .
Coenzymes with particular cofactors tend to catalyze similar transformations .
Coenzymes that contain pyridoxal pyrophosphate ( PLP ) , the active form of vitamin B6 , catalyze reactions that involve carbanionic intermediates.

Transimination-1
All PLP - containing coenzymes contain an imine bond between the protein and the cofactor in the resting state.
The imine is the result of a chemical reaction between PLP and a lysine residue in the enzyme active site .

Transimination-2
Reaction of the enzyme-bound PLP with an amino acid substrate exchanges one imine linkage for another. This is a transimination reaction .

Decarboxylation
The pyridine ring of PLP, especially when protonated, facilitates several kinds of reactions at the amino acid’s α carbon by acting as an electron-withdrawing group. One reaction is decarboxylation .
